headline: Micro-Credit Interest Rates at 18% Trap B40 Borrowers
context: Bank Negara's 2025 review found that micro-credit facilities offered by licensed moneylenders charged effective interest rates averaging 18% annually, trapping 480,000 B40 borrowers in revolving debt cycles.

cards.big: 480,000 B40 families pay 18% interest on micro-loans — 4x the home mortgage rate — for borrowing RM5,000 or less
cards.sub: Licensed moneylenders charge legal maximums that effectively penalise poverty — smaller loans carry highest rates

cards.big: Total micro-credit outstanding at RM4.8 billion with average loan size of RM3,200 — tiny amounts with huge interest burden
cards.sub: Borrowers pay RM860 million annually in interest alone — wealth transfer from poorest to licensed lenders

cards.big: 68% of micro-credit borrowers are women — single mothers and market traders using loans for daily business capital
cards.sub: Debt stress linked to 22% higher incidence of depression among female micro-credit borrowers per UKM health study

cards.big: Housing and Local Government Ministry licenses moneylenders but BNM sets no interest rate caps for non-bank lenders
cards.sub: Regulatory gap means micro-credit falls between two agencies — neither fully responsible for consumer protection

cards.big: Charging the poorest borrowers the highest interest rates isn't micro-finance — it's poverty extraction with a government licence
Should micro-credit interest rates be capped at 8% for loans under RM10,000 with government credit guarantee absorbing the risk?
